sentences.text: OK.
I was a born in, in a little town in Poland.
And it's Dabrowa Gornicza.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I was one child to my parents.
My father happened to be an orphan when he was five years old.
In a little town, an aunt raised him.
And for this reason, everything was really emotional, because whatever he did, he realized and he appreciate his life and, and being raised for my aunt.
So he became a philanthropist.
His whole life he was working for poor and collecting money and doing everything what is possible for the poor, for to get married of and get us what didn't have-- poor.
And, and he was the cashier from the Bikur Cholim, which was our organization for sick.
And he was-- he had never have time for himself because when he went to synagogue, he always brought for my mother the poor people to take care of, to sleep over, and to fed them.
And that's what he's always told me as a little girl, that you cannot take anything to your grave but your name.
So he was living up to this.
So my mother struggled a lot about this, because some of them wasn't as clean or put a cigarette in the fire in our house or tried to take something.
And we have a lot of-- as a child what was my observation.
And then I get older.
And I was one child.
I, I really-- What was the profession of your father?
My father start with tailoring.
And then in, in my time, he was selling materials, like fabric, fabric.
Yes.
I was born in, in June 21st, 21st, the first day of summer, 1921.
The attention what I had from my parents was doubled, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my parents lost in '17 or '16 or I think '13 and, and '14 a daughter and a son before me, in one day.
It was like here-- no problem in America.
Was diphtheria, what needs the penicillin.
And there was no cure in Poland this time so, one caught from the other.
And-- and-- and 10 years later, I was born.
So my father was a religious person.
And he always went to the rabbi.
And the rabbi gave him all instruction how to raise me, what kind name.
And I was seven years wearing white, white, white.
No other colors.
I was wearing all kind of superstitious [INAUDIBLE] all kind of things, seven years to my life.
That's what the rabbi prescribed, like I got the prescription.
And that's what my parents obey.
After seven years, I was permitted to put a dress with a little colored polka dots.
That was step by step, gradually.
And then they decide to give me the best Jewish education.
But being a Jew in Poland, you couldn't have, really, the higher education.
It was impossible.
So I reached to a bookkeeping school, private.
I was, I was public schooled.
And then I went private courses to Bedzin, to another city, for bookkeeping.
And I was going to a very orthodox Jewish school, like Bais Yaakov, Bais Yaakov, and-- and-- to become a teacher.
But because I was one child, my mother couldn't take it that I should study in a different town, not to see me.
And for this reason, I stopped this.
But while I was growing up, I had very good atmosphere of, of friends, very nice surrounding.
And I, I can say that I was lucky till the time came that the World, the Second World War broke out.
Before the war broke out--
-- I understood that I helped my parents in any direction in the house, in the profession.
Yeah.
No.
It was, it was the most-- because the Bedzin city contained from just the most Jewish people.
And I, I cannot recall that I was anything.
But when I was in public school, I had many very disappointments.
When I was little girl, when it comes Passover, then you have new clothes, which was a custom.
Yeah.
And non-Jewish kids passed by and spit at me or throw me a stone.
When I went to the religious school beside the, the main city, I had thrown a stone covered with snow in my hat.
I was very depressed.
The last days before the war broke out was impossible, was-- the anti-Semitism was rising in Poland very badly.
And our life was in, really, jeopardy because they were breaking the windows of the stores, of the Jewish stores.
And it was impossible.
So we are-- we were very used to a hard life, considering-- like, Holland or other countries, Belgium, when came to our concentration camps.
We were used to.
So we are the one, we, we could take more than the others.
The others usually, they failed after a few weeks.
They were dying out.
The nature of Poland Jews was very-- willpower.
A strong willpower we had.
Willpower to live.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Born willpower.
Yeah.
But me, as, as I was a one child, was really hard when I was taking-- I was working for the Wehrmacht, for the German.
OK.
OK.
We were-- when the war start, we looked out the window.
And we saw the synagogue is burning.
So the Germans came in.
And they just burned the synagogues.
They burn everything is possible and we felt.
So we knew that the things just doesn't look good.
And, and I was not concerned about my life.
I was this time, like, 17, 16.
I was just looking at my parents.
I couldn't believe it, what they're going through.
What's going to happen with us?
My father ran away to another city.
He was miraculous survived.
Because all the other people was thrown in the water.
And he, he was survived by cutting off his hair.
And he came back, but in a short time later, they were taking to-- this was not in my time.
Then I went to the concentration camp.
And they took me.
And I was supposed to be protected.
No.
I was home.
And I was protected working for Rosen.
This was like Schindler, a factory what makes uniforms for the Wehrmacht, for the, for the German soldiers.
So we thought that we not going to be taken in.
So we were so sure, but one Saturday--
Yeah.
Yes.
Not yet.
Yeah.
After--
How was the food?
There was no food.
I was still home.
But, but one Saturday, they made-- they came in, and everybody was taken to a [NON-ENGLISH] to another city, so Sosnowiec.
And from there they send us to, to Grunberg in Schlesien, which was Germany already.
By, by force, by the Gestapo.
The SS.
Yeah, probably by trucks.
I remember--
Not probably.
Tell me.
Do you remember?
Yeah.
I remember they took us--
But they have cattle cars or what?
--by train, by, by-- I, I don't remember exactly how they took us, because it was a time that the emotion, stress, and, and-- all young girls.
We were just starting life.
Yeah.
And my parents were still home.
But when we were working to go there to this, I remember my mother running after us and, and, and handing me a piece of cheesecake.
And then the SS man, I saw how he beat her up very badly.
She was falling.
And then she was spending all the time-- was January, was wintertime.
Was standing down, down there to look up.
And from all of this, she got pneumonia.
She was very sick.
Because I was already in concentration camp when other people came and told me this.
Grunberg Schlesien by Breslau.
Yeah.
No.
My mother was taken to Auschwitz.
She was 55.
My father was, my father was 55.
My mother, 50.
They were taken to Auschwitz.
That's all I know.
That was probably [BACKGROUND NOISE] a year after I was taken.
A year, probably, after I was taken.
They were taken to a ghetto first.
I wasn't there.
That was, I think, '40, '41, '42.
[INAUDIBLE]
It was, yeah.
Between Dabrowa and Bedzin was very, very short distance.
[SIGHS] And all-- in the beginning, my-- when I was in-- my parents still send me once in a while a piece of bread.
But it never reached me.
Because it was taken away or it was already stale.
I know, I know because the [NON-ENGLISH] what was leading us and was chosen to, to take care of us was a neighbor of, of ours.
So my, my parents went to the sister what she lives with, and, and they give her gold and whatever that she is good to me, that she gives me something, but it never, it never, it never worked.
Never worked.
I was very, very depressed.
So one time, an SS man from our camp came out and having-- to have fun.
What he did, he, he told us, who has complaints to come out and say.
And I was very naive.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] And I came, I came into concentration camp.
If we have complaints.
Just for fun.
So I said, just for the sake that I am one child to my parents, suffered so much-- I, I was too naive-- I would like to, to get out.
So I was beaten up.
And I was two weeks laying almost dead from-- swollen and black and blue.
And that was the fun of it.
They were laughing and making fun.
And I was a-- Yeah?
--a lot of people in the camp, 1,000 women.
Just women.
The, the last years came in few men--
--to another--
But in the beginning, it was just-- and when they beat you up, it was just women.
Just women.
We, we have one room, big, big, and we have three bed, one on the other.
And we were working a night shift or day shift.
We were making material for blankets for the Wehrmacht, for the soldiers.
We learned this profession in six weeks, which other German women, it took two or three years.
So he was very upset about it, this SS man.
And he said, [NON-ENGLISH] how come the Jewish are so-- can, can--
--learn so fast?
He was outrageous mad.
And for this reason, he came every Sunday.
He put us in a line.
And every 10th which came out had to go to Auschwitz to the, to the gas chamber.
Not one time I was the ninth or the 11th.
Yeah.
It was no secret.
No secret.
Yeah.
One, one time we had a lot of vance, that was little bugs would--
--eat us up alive.
Yeah.
So, so one time one of this came in in mine ear.
And I hardly could work because this was very precision work to weave on machines, material.
So I complained to the nurse, was a German nurse, and I said that I have something in my ear, and it's jumping a lot.
So she went to the SS man.
And she said that I'm foul, that I don't want to work.
So he had a big ring and he beat me up.
So I had also very swollen eyes, and I was all beat up because I didn't want to work, that's what they said.
But after many years, many days, God was good to me.
They took me to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- To the hospital.
And they put some water on it, and came out a live vance.
And I was relieved.
Yes.
For many days.
And it was buzzing me.
It was making me crazy.
They didn't believe it.
Because she was very, very mean, this nurse.
This was all kind of things what happened to me while I was there beside the hunger, beside the dirt.
The, the food-- they, they give a piece of bread, which maybe was three small slices, a little piece of margarine, and a soup what contains sand and, and you couldn't find a piece of potato.
You have to look.
So we always took out the piece of potato and left in a little dish for later to make a big, a big dinner from this.
This was all what we had in the daytime.
I don't know.
The clean was very bad.
We never had warm water.
It was five years.
Never warm water and not soap.
The soap was like, like, what you-- [INAUDIBLE] something-- made from I don't know what.
Was very bad.
And that's all.
I don't know how.
I don't know.
I was there.
I lived through.
And I can't believe myself.
We-- we didn't have special striped clothes, but I don't remember.
I really, I really don't remember the clothes.
I remember whatever we had, what we brought from the house, we, we had.
And after so many years of being just on this diet what I told you, I really cannot figure out how can a person live like this?
Because we, we didn't have any civilities to-- to-- to normal people, just like animals.
Just animals.
But somehow, God was with us.
That's-- from 1,000 girls, maybe 80 survived.
And from this 80, is just few left what I'm going to meet now in Prague.
Right to the factory, was taking us to the factory.
We work-- yeah.
We work almost 12 hours or something like this.
We had to work on, on a special clock what has to do as many, as many--
Pieces.
--pieces.
And, and if the, the thread didn't go and was turning too much, we were in trouble.
Because if you don't make this, you have the possibility to be sent to Auschwitz.
Everything was Auschwitz, Auschwitz, Auschwitz.
Nobody was killed there.
Yes.
Some people were a little sick, so they send them to Auschwitz.
They didn't bother anything.
Just, if you're sick, you go there.
Whole Poland was not too big.
Was close.
It was close from Grunberg to Auschwitz.
From Grunberg to Auschwitz was close.
Yeah.
And when a lot-- and some of them passed away, and some of them commit suicide, like put their heads down under the machine to finish.
Not everybody had the willpower to live.
So this camp, this factory, was part of Auschwitz.
Yeah, it was.
Yeah.
It was a part of Auschwitz.
Department, yeah.
Belonged to Auschwitz.
Yeah.
It was a department of Auschwitz.
And name was--
Grunberg Schlesien.
By Breslau.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very hard.
In the beginning, we, we received a few cards, mail, and then everything-- they, they, our parents was, all of them, sent to Auschwitz, and finito.
Was finished.
So we lived day by day and not too much hope to survive.
We were-- we knew we cannot survive this.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow we survived.
No.
I was-- OK, I was there in beginning till the end, until January '45.
In January of '45, they, they looked at themselves, and they decided that it's too close to-- it's going to happen, something.
And they decide to take us out and to, and to let us go with, with their-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they were on both sides of women and men, SS, and we were walking.
But we couldn't carry our big belongings, so we throw out, slowly, slowly, and we were left with just one thing.
And, and we approached a few countries.
That was winter without shoes.
I can't believe it.
Now people get colds.
We didn't get-- I didn't get a cold, even.
I was no shoes in snow, no food.
We were eating the snow on the way to-- I don't know.
When it came to night, they put us in a, a stable from, from the cows and not let--
That was good.
That was warm.
And not let-- not all the time-- not let us in to see there was machines.
We fell on, on on the points machines.
One or the other screaming, was all languages-- Hungarian, and, and, and it was just terrible.
March.
No.
That was all ours.
That was all your people.
And there were Hungarians there.
The last-- the last, the last few months, they brought some Hungarians.
But all the time was just the Polish neighborhood, like Bedzin, Sosnowiec, Katowice, and all them.
[NON-ENGLISH].
So we were walking.
And every day was less and less.
And they want to finish up that they can be free from, from their duties.
But somehow, they, they shoot a lot of them.
We had one time, I couldn't walk anymore.
My feet were swollen.
So I decide, whatever will be.
I saw-- I'm petite.
And I can fit in a doghouse.
So in the, in the light, in the morning, I decide I will run out to this doghouse.
And, and, and when he catch me, of course, he can shoot me.
So as a petite, I went into this doghouse.
And all of a sudden I see the, the [NON-ENGLISH].
He was a tall guy, SS man, with a small, a this small golden gun.
And he was shooting everybody.
So when I saw him coming, I said shema yisrael, and that's it.
And he said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He couldn't believe it, what I did.
Yeah.
Why did you run out in, in the light, in the daytime?
Couldn't you do this in the night, to, to run away from me?
He, he couldn't digest that, that I was so fresh, so, so impossible, bad.
So I, I said my prayer, and I wait till he shoot me.
I have nothing to lose.
So he, he said, all of a sudden, to, to run after the march.
This was--
After the other people.
Yeah.
This was because my father was-- he did so much good, I-- I-- I survive.
I believe in this, that my father's--
If you believe in it, it's a wonderful thing.
Yeah.
Yeah.
So anyway, I, I run after this.
And for being so excited, I had a little pillow or something in my hand that was my belonging.
So I dropped it.
And after maybe 10 minutes, I see him coming to, to the end of this march.
I was the last one.
And approaching me-- I said, now he's going to shoot me.
You know what?
He gave me this pillow.
This is such a miracle.
I can never forget this, as long as I live.
He shot, in my eyes, a lot of girls with his little golden gun.
And he didn't shoot me when he caught me running away, escaping.
How did you do exist with food on the march?
I don't know.
This is the question which I don't know.
Sometimes we found an old bone or a old piece of beets on the way when we marched.
We put it here.
And we lick and lick.
And from the snow.
But one time we had a party.
We went in--
We went in, in a, in a, in a stable.
And the French people were there before.
And they had baked potatoes, whatever, cooked potatoes.
And this, the pail was under straw.
So we had a party.
Everybody was eating this thing.
And then they became typhus, typhus, bloody typhus from all this cold, old potatoes.
And this was the only party we had through the five months.
So I like to make-- you have some questions.
Go ahead.
No, I don't have no questions.
OK.
So--
